Earliest Finish Time Priority Rule (EFT):
Step 1: Initialize the project network by identifying all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Create a list of all activities and calculate their earliest start times (EST) and earliest finish times (EFT) using forward pass scheduling
Step 3: Set the current time (t) to 0 and create an empty schedule
Step 4: Identify all activities that are available to be scheduled at the current time (all predecessors completed)
Step 5: Among available activities, select the activity with the smallest Earliest Finish Time (EFT) as the priority criterion
Step 6: Check if sufficient resources are available to schedule the selected activity at its earliest start time
Step 7: If resources are available, schedule the activity at its earliest start time and update resource availability
Step 8: If resources are not available, delay the activity and move to the next available activity in the priority list
Step 9: Update the current time to the next event time (earliest completion of a scheduled activity or earliest start of a waiting activity)
Step 10: Repeat steps 4-9 until all activities have been scheduled
Step 11: Calculate the project completion time (makespan) as the maximum finish time among all activities
Step 12: Verify that all resource constraints have been satisfied throughout the schedule
Step 13: Document the final schedule including start times, finish times, and resource allocations for each activity
Step 14: Analyze the schedule for optimization opportunities and compare with other priority rules if needed
Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
Initialize the project network with all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Set the current time (t) to 0 and create an empty schedule for all activities
Identify all activities that are eligible to be scheduled at time t (activities whose predecessors are all completed)
Among eligible activities, select the activity with the earliest start time (EST) value
If multiple activities have the same EST, apply a secondary priority rule (e.g., shortest duration, most critical path, or lowest activity ID)
Check if sufficient resources are available at time t to schedule the selected activity without exceeding resource capacity constraints
If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at time t and allocate the required resources
If resources are not available, move the activity to a waiting list and proceed to the next eligible activity
Update the current time t to the earliest time when a scheduled activity completes or when resources become available
Remove completed activities from the schedule and deallocate their resources
Repeat steps 3-10 until all activities are scheduled or no progress can be made
If activities remain unscheduled due to resource conflicts, apply backtracking or increase the project makespan and retry
Calculate the project completion time (makespan) as the maximum finish time among all activities
Generate the final schedule showing start times, finish times, and resource allocations for each activity
Validate the schedule to ensure all precedence constraints and resource constraints are satisfied
Latest Finish Time Priority Rule (LFT):
Step 1: Initialize the project network by identifying all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Calculate the project completion time (makespan) using forward pass scheduling to determine the critical path
Step 3: Perform backward pass scheduling to calculate the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity based on the project completion time
Step 4: Calculate the Latest Start Time (LST) for each activity using the formula: LST = LFT - Duration
Step 5: Create a priority list by sorting all activities in non-decreasing order of their Latest Finish Times (LFT values)
Step 6: Initialize the resource availability levels for each resource type at time zero
Step 7: Set the current scheduling time (t) to zero and create a list of schedulable activities
Step 8: Identify all activities that are ready to be scheduled at time t (all predecessors completed and no scheduling conflicts)
Step 9: From the schedulable activities, select the activity with the smallest LFT value according to the priority list
Step 10: Check if sufficient resources are available to schedule the selected activity at time t
Step 11: If resources are available, schedule the activity at time t and update resource availability by subtracting the activity's resource requirements
Step 12: If resources are not available, move to the next time point when resources become available and repeat the resource check
Step 13: Update the current time t to the earliest time when an activity completes or when the next schedulable activity can start
Step 14: Remove completed activities from the schedulable list and update resource availability by adding back their released resources
Step 15: Repeat steps 8-14 until all activities have been scheduled
Step 16: Calculate the final project makespan (completion time of the last activity)
Step 17: Validate the schedule by verifying that all precedence constraints are satisfied and resource constraints are not violated
Step 18: Output the final schedule including start times, finish times, and resource allocations for each activity
Latest Start Time Priority Rule (LST):
Step 1: Calculate the project completion time (makespan) using forward pass scheduling to determine the earliest finish time of the project
Step 2: Perform backward pass scheduling from the project completion time to calculate the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity
Step 3: Calculate the Latest Start Time (LST) for each activity using the formula: LST = LFT - Duration
Step 4: Initialize the schedule by setting the current time (t) to 0 and marking all activities as unscheduled
Step 5: At each time step t, identify all activities that are available for scheduling (all predecessors have been completed)
Step 6: Among available activities, select the activity with the smallest Latest Start Time (LST) value as the priority rule
Step 7: Check if sufficient resources are available to schedule the selected activity at time t without violating resource constraints
Step 8: If resources are available, schedule the selected activity to start at time t and update resource availability
Step 9: If resources are insufficient, defer the activity and check the next priority activity in the LST order
Step 10: If no activities can be scheduled at time t due to resource constraints, advance time to the next resource availability point
Step 11: Update the completion status of activities that finish at time t and free up their allocated resources
Step 12: Repeat steps 5-11 until all activities have been scheduled
Step 13: Calculate the final project makespan (total project duration) from the schedule
Step 14: Validate the schedule by verifying that all precedence constraints and resource constraints are satisfied
Step 15: Output the final schedule with start times, finish times, and resource allocations for each activity
Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MS):
Step 1: Initialize the project network by reading all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Identify all activities with no predecessors and add them to the available activities list
Step 3: Calculate the earliest start time (EST) and earliest finish time (EFT) for all activities using forward pass scheduling
Step 4: Calculate the latest start time (LST) and latest finish time (LFT) for all activities using backward pass scheduling
Step 5: Calculate the slack (float) for each activity as: Slack = LST - EST (or LFT - EFT)
Step 6: Initialize the current time (t) to 0 and create a resource availability vector for each resource type
Step 7: At each time step, identify all activities that are eligible to start (all predecessors completed and current time >= EST)
Step 8: Among eligible activities, select the activity with the minimum slack value as the priority criterion
Step 9: If multiple activities have the same minimum slack, apply a secondary priority rule (e.g., shortest duration, lowest activity ID)
Step 10: Check if the selected activity's resource requirements can be satisfied with available resources at current time
Step 11: If resources are available, schedule the activity to start at current time and update resource availability
Step 12: If resources are not available, defer the activity and try the next highest priority activity
Step 13: Advance time to the next event (either an activity completion or the earliest start time of a waiting activity)
Step 14: Update the list of completed activities and release their resources back to the resource pool
Step 15: Repeat steps 7-14 until all activities are scheduled
Step 16: Calculate the project makespan (total project duration) as the maximum finish time among all activities
Step 17: Output the schedule including start times, finish times, and resource allocations for each activity
Step 18: Validate the schedule by verifying all precedence constraints and resource constraints are satisfied
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Initialize the project network by reading all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Create a list of all activities and mark them as unscheduled initially
Step 3: Set the current time (t) to 0 and initialize resource availability levels for each resource type
Step 4: Identify all activities that are eligible for scheduling at the current time (all predecessors completed)
Step 5: For each eligible activity, calculate the total resource demand (sum of all resource requirements weighted by duration or intensity)
Step 6: Select the activity with the Greatest Resource Demand (GRD) among all eligible activities
Step 7: Schedule the selected activity at the earliest feasible time, checking that required resources are available
Step 8: If resources are insufficient, move to the next time point and repeat resource availability check
Step 9: Update the resource availability by subtracting the scheduled activity's resource requirements
Step 10: Mark the scheduled activity as completed and update its finish time
Step 11: Move to the next time point (earliest completion time of any scheduled activity)
Step 12: Repeat steps 4-11 until all activities are scheduled
Step 13: Calculate the project makespan (total project duration) as the maximum finish time among all activities
Step 14: Verify that all precedence constraints and resource constraints are satisfied
Step 15: Output the final schedule with activity start times, finish times, and resource allocation
Most Successors Priority Rule (MS):
Step 1: Initialize the project network by reading all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Create a list of all activities and calculate the number of successors for each activity (direct and indirect successors)
Step 3: Set the current time (t) to 0 and create a schedule list to store scheduled activities
Step 4: Identify all activities that are currently available for scheduling (activities whose predecessors have been completed)
Step 5: Among available activities, select the activity with the maximum number of successors (Most Successors rule)
Step 6: If there is a tie (multiple activities with the same maximum number of successors), apply a secondary priority rule (e.g., shortest duration, earliest start time, or activity ID)
Step 7: Check if sufficient resources are available at time t to schedule the selected activity
Step 8: If resources are available, schedule the activity at time t and update the resource availability
Step 9: If resources are not available, move to the next available time point when resources become free and repeat Step 8
Step 10: Update the completion time of the scheduled activity as t plus its duration
Step 11: Remove the scheduled activity from the available activities list
Step 12: Update the list of available activities by checking if any newly freed activities now have all predecessors completed
Step 13: Move the current time (t) to the earliest time when a resource becomes available or when a new activity can start
Step 14: Repeat Steps 4-13 until all activities have been scheduled
Step 15: Calculate the project makespan (total project duration) as the maximum completion time among all activities
Step 16: Output the final schedule including activity start times, end times, resource allocations, and project makespan
Step 17: Validate the schedule by verifying all precedence constraints are satisfied and resource constraints are not violated
Most Predecessors Priority Rule (MP):
Initialize the project network with all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Create a list of all activities and set their earliest start times (EST) and earliest finish times (EFT) using forward pass scheduling
Calculate the number of predecessors for each activity (including transitive predecessors)
Initialize the current time (t) to 0 and create a set of scheduled activities (empty initially)
Create a list of available activities at the current time (activities with all predecessors scheduled)
For each available activity, calculate its priority based on the number of predecessors (higher number = higher priority)
Select the activity with the highest number of predecessors from the available activities list
Check if sufficient resources are available at the current time to schedule the selected activity
If resources are available, schedule the activity at the current time and add it to the scheduled activities set
If resources are not available, move to the next time point and repeat resource availability check
Update the resource availability by subtracting the resources used by the newly scheduled activity
Remove the scheduled activity from the available activities list
Update the list of available activities by checking which activities now have all predecessors scheduled
Increment the current time to the next relevant time point (earliest start time of remaining activities or finish time of scheduled activities)
Repeat steps 5-13 until all activities are scheduled
Calculate the project makespan (total project duration) as the maximum finish time among all activities
Verify that all precedence constraints and resource constraints are satisfied
Output the schedule including activity start times, finish times, and resource allocation over time
Shortest Processing Time Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Initialize the project network by identifying all activities, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Create a list of all activities with their attributes including duration, predecessors, successors, and resource demands
Step 3: Set the current time (t) to 0 and mark all activities as unscheduled
Step 4: Identify all activities that are currently available for scheduling (all predecessors completed)
Step 5: Among available activities, select the activity with the shortest processing time (duration) as the priority criterion
Step 6: Check if sufficient resources are available at the current time to schedule the selected activity
Step 7: If resources are available, schedule the activity at time t and update resource availability
Step 8: If resources are not available, move to the next available time when resources become free and repeat Step 6
Step 9: Update the completion time of the scheduled activity as t + activity duration
Step 10: Mark the scheduled activity as completed and remove it from the unscheduled list
Step 11: Update the list of available activities by checking which activities now have all predecessors completed
Step 12: Increment time t to the earliest time when a resource becomes available or an activity completes
Step 13: Repeat Steps 4-12 until all activities are scheduled
Step 14: Calculate the project makespan (total project duration) as the maximum completion time among all activities
Step 15: Validate the schedule by verifying all precedence constraints are satisfied
Step 16: Validate the schedule by verifying resource constraints are not violated at any time period
Step 17: Document the final schedule including start times, end times, and resource allocations for each activity
Step 18: Generate a Gantt chart visualization showing the activity timeline and resource utilization
Step 19: Calculate performance metrics such as resource utilization rate, idle time, and schedule efficiency
Step 20: Compare the SPT solution with other priority rules or heuristics to evaluate solution quality
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
Step 1: Initialize the project network by reading all tasks, their durations, precedence relationships, and resource requirements
Step 2: Create a list of all tasks and sort them in descending order by processing time (duration), with ties broken arbitrarily or by task ID
Step 3: Initialize the current time (t) to 0 and create resource availability arrays tracking remaining capacity for each resource type at each time period
Step 4: Create a set of schedulable tasks (tasks with all predecessors completed) and initialize it with tasks that have no predecessors
Step 5: While there are unscheduled tasks remaining, proceed to the next step
Step 6: Identify the task with the longest processing time from the schedulable tasks set
Step 7: Check if sufficient resources are available at the current time (t) to schedule this task
Step 8: If resources are available, schedule the task at time t, update resource availability by subtracting the task's resource requirements, and add the task's successors to the schedulable tasks set
Step 9: If resources are not available, increment the current time (t) to the next time point when resources become available (earliest resource release time)
Step 10: After scheduling a task or advancing time, re-sort the schedulable tasks by processing time in descending order
Step 11: Remove the scheduled task from the schedulable tasks set
Step 12: Update the list of schedulable tasks by adding any newly available tasks whose predecessors are now complete
Step 13: Repeat steps 6-12 until all tasks are scheduled
Step 14: Calculate the project makespan (total project duration) as the maximum finish time among all tasks
Step 15: Output the schedule including task start times, finish times, and resource allocation over time
Step 16: Validate the schedule by verifying all precedence constraints are satisfied and resource constraints are not violated
